channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: As Russia looks to slash budgets, a village fights to survive
item.description: Officials now openly blame the war in Ukraine for cuts to services

item.title: The World Is Paying the Price for America’s War
item.description: Conflict with Iran hurts American wallets, but it’s far more devastating for people in the global south.

item.title: How Likely Are U.S.-Iran Peace Talks in Islamabad?
item.description: The U.S. seizure of an Iranian ship threatens to upend the planned negotiations.

item.title: American corporate profits keep shrugging off global tumult
item.description: Earnings expectations are through the roof

item.title: How Big of a Threat Is Mythos?
item.description: Anthropic’s latest AI model has kick-started a new debate.

item.title: The Strategic Aftershocks of Trump’s Iran War
item.description: The consequences will be felt long after the fighting ends.

item.title: Why Trump Cannot Walk Away From Canada
item.description: Trump needs Ottawa more than he’ll say.

item.title: Albertans find it harder than expected to break from Canada. Good
item.description: Separatists in the oil-rich province are beset by political and legal challenges

item.title: Back to Basics at the U.N.
item.description: Rather than climate, disease, or artificial intelligence, the next secretary-general should stay focused on conflict resolution.

item.title: The Geopolitics of Infrastructure
item.description: No longer does global power rest only on alliances, military might, currency dynamics, and effective control of multilateral institutions. The new geopolitical contest is between competing infrastructure blocs: packages of finance, contractors, standards, and data systems that create long-term dependencies.

item.title: Chernobyl’s forgotten nuclear lessons
item.description: Forty years after the disaster, the power plant still poses a threat

item.title: Torture by Design? Why India’s Laws Are Not Enough
item.description: Custodial violence is not episodic but rather structural, woven into the daily architecture of incarceration.

item.title: Australia-Japan Frigate Deal Faces 3 Critical Challenges
item.description: Australia’s frigate purchase will likely end up being more complicated than the “zero-change” framing suggests.

item.title: The world wants Chinese tech. China is determined to keep it
item.description: China’s rivals are learning how to get what China won’t share

item.title: Africa’s Geopolitical Hand Is Stronger than Ever
item.description: As the old world order fractures and a multipolar era takes shape, Africa has an unprecedented opportunity to translate its global position and enviable economic endowments into durable geopolitical and economic leverage. But African leaders will have to play their cards right.

item.title: North Korea Fires 5 Tactical Ballistic Missiles
item.description: Pyongyang fired “surface-to-surface” missile launches roughly one month before U.S. President Donald Trump’s visit to Beijing.

item.title: China Needs to Exercise Caution With Economic Penalties
item.description: China should avoid treating long-established external economic ties as instruments that can be readily deployed or withdrawn.

item.title: Setting Realistic Expectations for Kazakhstan’s Regional Ecological Summit
item.description: The Regional Ecological Summit could kickstart a wider climate activism campaign in the greater Central Asian region, or it could just be a short-term, pro-green mirage.

item.title: The Private Credit Panic Is Overblown
item.description: Private credit markets are under growing stress, fueling fears of a financial crisis that could spill over to the real economy. But a closer look at the evidence suggests that the risks are less severe than in previous cycles, and that predictions of a meltdown are running well ahead of the facts.

item.title: Why China Wants to Host the New BBNJ Secretariat
item.description: China’s Xiamen bid ups the stakes in the the battle over the secretariat host.

item.title: Australia-Japan Ink Deal for Mogami-class Frigates
item.description: Canberra and Tokyo continue to build closer links with each other, with a shared understanding of the strategic environment, and a high degree of trust

item.title: Hormuz Today, Taiwan Tomorrow
item.description: By closing the Strait of Hormuz, Iran has handed the Trump administration a practice test. To pass—and preserve deterrence against a Chinese invasion or blockade of Taiwan—the United States must reopen the Strait decisively and visibly with escorts, minesweepers, and strikes on launch sites.

item.title: Lakshadweep’s Fishermen Are India’s First Line of Maritime Intelligence
item.description: Fishing communities notice everything. But alienation from Delhi means India is losing intelligence input it can’t afford to lose.

item.title: The 500th Anniversary of the First Battle of Panipat
item.description: Contrary to popular belief, the Mughals did not bring Islamic rule to India — but their rule did shape India’s military, administrative, and geopolitical landscape in ways that endure to this day.

item.title: What Really Drives China’s Massive Trade Surplus
item.description: China’s trade surplus is often blamed on its industrial policies. In reality, however, it reflects a persistent gap between savings and investment, driven by demographic pressures and financial constraints that shape household behavior and restrict private firms’ access to credit.

item.title: Can Trump Export Zambia’s HIV Success?
item.description: Years of investment made certain provinces resilient to aid cuts, but replicating that system is another story.

item.title: Anduril, Palantir and SpaceX are changing how America wages war
item.description: The Trump administration is cosying up to a clique of “neo-primes”

item.title: Why the Next Generation of Republicans Might Be More Extreme Than MAGA
item.description: President Trump could come to represent the restrained, reasonable wing of the GOP.

item.title: How Vietnam’s ‘Four Nos’ Can Help a Region Manage the U.S. Rogue Use of Force
item.description: The country's preference for an open and predicable international order is shared by most of its Asian neighbors.

item.title: South Korea’s President Set to Pay 4-Day State Visit To Vietnam
item.description: Lee Jae Myung is the first foreign leader to visit Vietnam since the confirmation of the country's new leadership earlier this month.

item.title: Can Saudi Arabia Keep Hedging?
item.description: The Iran war has altered the Gulf’s balance of power—and the kingdom’s calculus.

item.title: The G-2’s Missing Link
item.description: Why America and China need ASEAN.

item.title: New Myanmar Administration Releases Ousted President Win Myint in Mass Amnesty
item.description: Newly inaugurated President Min Aung Hlaing also announced a reduction in the 27-year prison sentence of ousted former leader Aung San Suu Kyi.

item.title: From Malaysia to the World: The Asian Legacy of Pope Francis
item.description: The canonization process for Sybil Kathigasu underscores how Pope Francis’s legacy continues to shift the lines of Catholicism, in subtle and surprising ways.
